sound_type: rattling wing-whistle in flight -- mechanical sound produced by narrow outer primary feathers during flight, 1000-4000 Hz, continuous during flight, involuntary, audible at 50-200 meters, E=4, 'the whistler'
ecosystem: Lake and coastal waters -- breeds in boreal forest across North America and Eurasia. Winters on large lakes, rivers, and coastal waters including Puget Sound. Cavity nester using tree cavities and nest boxes.
season: Winter visitor to PNW. Breeds in boreal forest (Alaska to Newfoundland). Arrives on Puget Sound waters in fall, departs in spring. Wing-whistle audible throughout winter presence.

coordinates.description: Reference for winter habitat on Puget Sound. Common Goldeneye winters in sheltered marine waters, bays, and freshwater lakes throughout the PNW lowlands.

taxonomy_note.description: Seventh consecutive Anatidae (118-124). One degree short of tying Picidae nonet record (9 consecutive). Completes comparison pair with Barrow's Goldeneye (degree 123). Third and final member of the Bucephala trilogy (Bufflehead 122, Barrow's 123, Common 124). Species name clangula from Latin clangere ('to resound') referring to the wing-whistle. Common name 'whistler' is centuries-old.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae count: 12+ entries across the engine. Seven consecutive at degrees 118-124.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Bucephala from Greek bous ('ox') + kephale ('head') -- 'ox-headed.' clangula from Latin clangere ('to resound, to clang') -- 'the resounding one,' referring to the wing-whistle. The entire naming apparatus points to the mechanical sound.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Bucephala -- third entry completing the trilogy: Bufflehead (122), Barrow's Goldeneye (123), Common Goldeneye (124). All three North American Bucephala documented.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae at seventh consecutive. One short of tying Picidae record (9). Two more would tie. Three would break it.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 1,000-4,000 Hz. The wing-whistle is upper-mid frequency, above most duck vocalizations. Produced by air over narrow outer primary feathers.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1,500-3,000 Hz. Peak whistle energy concentrated in the upper midrange.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Wing-whistle: 1,000-4,000 Hz. Male courtship call: buzzy nasal peeent at 500-2,000 Hz (secondary to wing-whistle).
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Continuous during flight. Each wingbeat produces a whistle pulse. Flight may last seconds to minutes. The sound is unceasing for the duration of active flight.
song_metrics.note_structure: MECHANICAL WING-WHISTLE: Rattling, singing whistle synchronized with wingbeat. Each downstroke produces slightly different pitch than upstroke, creating characteristic rattling quality. Not a vocalization -- produced by feather structure.
song_metrics.note_types: Wing-whistle (primary, mechanical, involuntary during flight), male courtship peeent (vocal, secondary), female harsh croak (vocal). Wing-whistle is the defining sound.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Continuous pulsing whistle matching wingbeat frequency (8-10 wingbeats/second). No phrases -- continuous mechanical production during flight.
song_metrics.timbre: Rattling. Singing. High-pitched. The whistle has a modulated, pulsing character rather than a pure tone. The narrow outer primaries produce a complex spectral signature with overtones.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Moderate to loud. Audible at 50-200 meters in calm conditions. Carries well over water. Not as loud as the Pileated's drum (E=6) or the Harlequin's stream calls (E=5), but distinctly audible at moderate distance.
song_metrics.tempo: 8-10 wingbeats per second in cruising flight. The whistle pulses at this frequency -- a rapid, continuous rhythm.
song_metrics.context: FLIGHT -- ANY FLIGHT. The wing-whistle is involuntary. The bird cannot turn it off. Every time a Common Goldeneye flies, it whistles. This means the sound is heard during takeoff, landing, transit between feeding areas, courtship flights, and escape flights. The wing-whistle is the most omnipresent sound in Common Goldeneye ecology.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. The wing-whistle is determined by feather structure, not learned behavior. Consistent across the entire Holarctic range.
song_metrics.key_property: RATTLING WING-WHISTLE IN FLIGHT -- mechanical sound from narrow outer primaries. E=4. 1,000-4,000 Hz. Involuntary, continuous during flight. Audible at 50-200m. 'The whistler' -- centuries-old nickname. Clangula = 'the resounding one.' First species defined by fully mechanical sound. COMPLETES COMPARISON PAIR with Barrow's (123): round spot vs. crescent, green vs. purple, wing-whistle vs. grunts, cosmopolitan vs. specialist. SEVENTH CONSECUTIVE ANATIDAE (118-124). COMPLETES BUCEPHALA TRILOGY (122-124).

plumage.overall: Male: black and white diving duck with distinctive ROUND white facial spot between eye and bill. Head has greenish gloss in good light. Back has extensive white scapular patches -- more white than Barrow's. Female: brown head, gray body, yellow-tipped bill.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Male: ROUND white spot (vs. Barrow's crescent). Greenish head gloss (vs. Barrow's purple). Rounder, more smoothly contoured head (vs. Barrow's angular). More extensive white scapulars. Female: longer, more gradually tapering bill. More gradual forehead slope.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Eclipse plumage in summer -- males resemble females. Non-breeding plumage less distinctive.
plumage.flight_pattern: Fast, direct flight with rapid wingbeats. DISTINCTIVE WING-WHISTLE audible throughout flight. The whistle is the primary flight identification cue -- hear the whistler before seeing it.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized diving duck. Body length 42-50 cm. Wingspan 65-80 cm. Weight 600-1300 g. Similar size to Barrow's Goldeneye with substantial overlap.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Round white facial spot on male. Greenish head gloss. Rounder head shape than Barrow's. Wing-whistle in flight. Golden yellow iris (the 'goldeneye').
physical_description.field_id: Listen first: the rattling wing-whistle in flight is unmistakable. Then look for: round (not crescent) white facial spot on male; greenish (not purple) head gloss; rounder head shape; more extensive white scapulars. On winter Puget Sound, compare directly with Barrow's in mixed flocks.

nodes.title: The Wing-Whistle -- Mechanical Sound Production
nodes.description: Rattling whistle from narrow outer primary feathers. 1,000-4,000 Hz. Involuntary, continuous during flight. Audible at 50-200m. First species defined by a fully mechanical sound. 'The whistler.' Clangula = 'the resounding one.'

nodes.title: Round Spot vs. Crescent -- Comparison Pair Complete
nodes.description: Common (round spot, green gloss, wing-whistle E=4, cosmopolitan 2.5-4.6M) vs. Barrow's (crescent, purple gloss, grunts E=3, restricted 150-200K). Two acoustic strategies within one genus. Mirrors Hairy/Downy WP pair (110-111).

nodes.title: Mechanical Sound Connection -- Feathers and Bills
nodes.description: Common Goldeneye (wing-whistle, feather structure) joins Pileated Woodpecker (drum, bill strikes) as species producing defining non-vocal sounds. Two mechanical sound-production strategies: aerodynamic (feather) and percussive (bill).

nodes.title: Bucephala Trilogy Complete -- Three Ox-Headed Divers (122-124)
nodes.description: Bufflehead (122, smallest), Barrow's Goldeneye (123, most restricted), Common Goldeneye (124, most widespread). All three North American Bucephala documented in consecutive degrees.

nodes.title: Seventh Consecutive Anatidae (118-124)
nodes.description: Harlequin (118), Common Merganser (119), Hooded Merganser (120), Wood Duck (121), Bufflehead (122), Barrow's Goldeneye (123), Common Goldeneye (124). Seven consecutive. One short of tying Picidae nonet record (9).

cross_references.relationship: COMPARISON PAIR! Common (round spot, green gloss, wing-whistle E=4, cosmopolitan 2.5-4.6M) vs. Barrow's (crescent, purple gloss, grunts E=3, restricted 150-200K). Two acoustic strategies within one genus.
BUCEPHALA TRILOGY COMPLETE! Bufflehead (122, smallest), Barrow's (123, most restricted), Common (124, most widespread). All three North American Bucephala in three consecutive degrees.

MECHANICAL SOUND CONNECTION! Both produce defining non-vocal sounds. Pileated: bill strikes on dead snag (percussion, 40-80 Hz). Common Goldeneye: air over feathers (aerodynamic whistle, 1,000-4,000 Hz). Two mechanical strategies.
ANATIDAE STREAK! Seven consecutive (118-124). Harlequin started the streak at E=5. Common Goldeneye at degree 124 is seven consecutive. One short of tying Picidae record.

CAVITY NESTERS! Both cavity-nesting Anatidae. Common Goldeneye readily uses nest boxes -- an important conservation tool. Wood Duck also uses nest boxes. Shared nesting strategy across the Anatidae streak.
